issue_title: add support for parsing JSON
issue_body:
Sometimes I'm dealing with deeply nested JSON and I'd like to parse it with xpaths. I know I can use JMESPATH but I dont like JMESPATH syntax, I'm used to xpaths. Also seems like some features of xpaths are not supported by Jmespath, e.g. I can't do recursive traversal, so I can't write `//mynode/text()` in Jmes I have to do `root.foo.bar[].foo2.bar2.product.mynode`.

It seems like JSON is currently not supported by js2xml, e.g. something like this:

```python
import js2xml
import json

alfa = json.dumps({"aa": "bb"})
print(alfa)
parsed = js2xml.parse(alfa)
print(parsed.pretty_print())
```
fails. 

Is there some way to add support for JSON parser to js2xml? Will this be useful thing in this project? This would probably require conversion from Python dictionary to xml, seems like there are packages that do this https://github.com/delfick/python-dict2xml so maybe we could learn something from them.

issue_title: Producer linger_ms=0 blocks gevent
issue_body:
**PyKafka version**: 2.8.0dev2 (master) (but any would do)
**Kafka version**: 0.10+, 1.0+ (but any would do)

I am using Gevent handler.
To flush messages right away, I set `linger_ms=0` in producer. When there is no message, it keeps flushing in queue_reader and effectively blocks the whole program. I can imagine that the threaded version will not really "block" but a high CPU usage may be observed.

I realize that to flush when there is at least 1 message, `min_queued_messages=1` should be used. 

However, the inline comment says:

> linger_ms=0 indicates no lingering.

without indicating the consequences, which may be misleading. If there is no plan to change the flush loop, I suggest adding a note of the consequences of `linger_ms=0`.

issue_title: Messages lost when broker closes connection
issue_body:
Already queued messages are lost when a SocketDisconnectError is detected [here](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/56efe3975ee707f4ec48aa8da81fde9fbbf14dda/pykafka/producer.py#L518).

This is occurring because the `Producer._update` method first closes all OwnedBrokers [here](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/56efe3975ee707f4ec48aa8da81fde9fbbf14dda/pykafka/producer.py#L274), which results in the OwnedBroker setting `self.running` to False [here](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/56efe3975ee707f4ec48aa8da81fde9fbbf14d.da/pykafka/producer.py#L632). Then, `Producer._update` calls `Producer._setup_owned_brokers` to reestablish the broker connection.

`Producer._setup_owned_brokers` (which also does it's own `OwnedBroker.stop` [call](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/56efe3975ee707f4ec48aa8da81fde9fbbf14dda/pykafka/producer.py#L294)) calls `OwnedBrokers.flush` to collect all currently queued messages [here](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/56efe3975ee707f4ec48aa8da81fde9fbbf14dda/pykafka/producer.py#L299).

The attempt to collect all currently queued messages will never return any message, because `OwnedBroker.flush` checks to see if `self.running` is False and if so, returns an empty list [here](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/56efe3975ee707f4ec48aa8da81fde9fbbf14dda/pykafka/producer.py#L698). `self.running` will be False, because that OwnedBroker was stopped.

I discovered this in a long running test that in the course of queuing 3,200,000 messages, a couple of dozen messages would go missing.

issue_title: BalancedConsumer is not auto balanced
issue_body:
In case of one topic with one partition, if two balanced consumers are created within the same group for the topic, the later one would raise exception.  When the first balanced consumer is down, messages are not consumed anymore

However, in Java kafka client, the later kafka consumer would continue to consume the topic's messages.  The behavior is more resilient.

issue_title: Possible to have more descriptive errors when deleting topics
issue_body:
Just encountered a scenario where I was trying to delete a topic like this: 

```
cl = pykafka.KafkaClient(...)
test_topic = cl.topics['test'.encode()] 
cl.cluster.controller_broker.delete_topics(['test'.encode()], timeout=1000)
```
and got a `SocketDisconnectedError`. Eventually tracked the problem down to topic deletion being disabled in the server config. Not sure if it's feasible to have a more descriptive error here, but it would be useful sometimes.

issue_body:
Assumes https://git-lfs.github.com/ is installed.

If a student submits a large file (e.g., `101.bin`) and then re-submits their work with that file removed and perhaps another big file added (e.g., `102.bin`), the second submission fails because `101.bin` is not actually checked out, since we do a bare clone (to avoid downloading files unnecessarily). Symptom is avoided by adding `--no-verify` to the subsequent push, but that seems to disable Git LFS's pre-push hook altogether, which probably isn't ideal. 

# simulates first submission

```
$ mkdir ~/git_work_tree1

$ cd ~/git_work_tree1

$ git init

$ git remote add origin git@github.com:cs50/lfs-demo.git

Git LFS initialized.

1+0 records out

1+0 records in
1+0 records out
105906176 bytes (106 MB) copied, 0.106539 s, 994 MB/s

Tracking "101.bin"

$ git add 1.bin 101.bin .gitattributes

$ git commit -m 1
[master (root-commit) a54e5d7] 1
 3 files changed, 4 insertions(+)
 create mode 100644 .gitattributes

 create mode 100644 101.bin

Git LFS: (1 of 1 files) 101.00 MB / 101.00 MB

To github.com:cs50/lfs-demo.git

Branch master set up to track remote branch master from origin.

```
$ mkdir ~/git_dir2

issue_title: fix style per PEP
debug option to client works oddly (and doesn't work in python2.6)
issue_body:
When you create an instance of the `Client` class and pass `debug=True`, the `__init__` method will execute

    self.log.setLevel('DEBUG')

and there are 2 problems with this.

1) `self.log` is the logger instance named 'segment' - it's the same instance for every client, so if you create multiple clients you can't use different values for `debug` or things get confused.

2) In python 2.6, you can't say `log.setLevel('DEBUG')`, you have to say `log.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)`, otherwise you're effectively setting the log level to an arbitrary high level and no log messages appear.

Is python 2.6 supported? I can't find any documentation that specifies either way.

issue_title: Document "meta", "visual" and "vizmeta" for regions
issue_body:
I saw that all regions (e.g. [CircleSkyRegion](http://astropy-regions.readthedocs.io/en/latest/_modules/regions/shapes/circle.html#CircleSkyRegion)) have `meta` and `visual` arguments in `__init__` and data members:

```
    def __init__(self, center, radius, meta=None, visual=None):
        # TODO: test that center is a 0D SkyCoord
        self.center = center
        self.radius = radius
        self.meta = meta or {}
        self.visual = visual or {}
```

As far as I know these are not documented anywhere (not mentioned as parameters in the docstring), not included in the str representation of the region and not explained here:
http://astropy-regions.readthedocs.io/en/latest/getting_started.html#ds9

I tried this one example ... in this case apparently an extra `vizmeta` attribute is populated with info from the DS9 file:

``` python
>>> from regions import read_ds9
>>> regions = read_ds9('regions/io/tests/data/ds9.color.reg')
>>> regions[0].__dict__
{'center': <FK5 Coordinate (equinox=J2000.000): (ra, dec) in deg
     (202.46947917, 47.19583889)>,
 'meta': {'include': ''},
 'radius': <Quantity 1.0 arcsec>,
 'visual': {},
 'vizmeta': {'color': 'blue'}}
```

Personally my motivation to work on the DS9 aspect of `regions` is small, I want to focus on using regions for analysis, i.e. implement polygons etc.

@keflavich or anyone else familiar with `regions/io` -- could you please write up a page of info and examples that explain how the I/O and these attributes work?
Either extend http://astropy-regions.readthedocs.io/en/latest/getting_started.html#ds9 or better yet, just make a new separate page in the high-level docs.
That could also include a comparison with `pyregion`, how it represents the info from ds9 files and how `regions` is similar or different.

issue_title: EllipsePixelRegion Angle argument is in radians, not degrees
issue_body:
When defining an EllipsePixelRegion, the angle argument is specified in degrees, but performs rotations as if it is in radians. 

For example:
`EllipsePixelRegion(center, major_axis, minor_axis, angle=90*u.deg)` returns an ellipse with a rotation angle of 90 _radians_.
`EllipsePixelRegion(center, major_axis, minor_axis, angle=1.57*u.deg)` returns an ellipse with a rotation angle of 90 degrees (1.57 radians).

issue_title: Possible issues with API compatibility in dependencies
issue_body:
Consider the following scenario: I have an affiliated package **A** which depends on other affiliated packages **B** and **C**. The packages **B** and **C** are listed in `install_requires` in my `setup.py` file. Now, in a clean environment, I run a command like `setup.py install`.

In this particular scenario, it appears to be the case that the instance of `astropy_helpers` that is packaged with **A** will be used to build the dependencies **B** and **C** as well. This would make sense if, as I suspect, `setuptools` is running the entire build/installation process from within a single Python process.

The problem is that the dependencies **B** and **C** probably came from PyPI, and so they may not have the latest and greatest version of `astropy_helpers`. Since my package is being built and installed from source, it probably does have a later version of `astropy_helpers`.

In certain cases, this can cause build failures. Consider the submodule `astropy_helpers.test_helpers`, which was previously deprecated, and was recently removed. If dependency **B** makes reference to `test_helpers` in its `setup.py` file, it will fail to build if package **A** has the latest version of `astropy_helpers`.

"So what?", you may rightly ask. There are plenty of workarounds, like pip requirements files or conda environments where all dependencies are installed before running a command like `setup.py install`. That's fair, and I'm not sure what the right solution is anyway. At the very least I just wanted to make a record of this issue since I am confident that someone else will run into it at some point in the future.

issue_title: peak_local_max should have saner defaults
issue_body:
While I understand the usefulness of `threshold_abs` and `threshold_rel`, it seems that defaults of respectively `-np.inf` and `None` (ie., don't use)  would be much more reasonable (`-np.inf` as default for `threshold_rel` would fail if the values in the image were all negative...).  It seems to me that when one calls a function named `peak_local_max`, one expects it to just return local maxima, not to add on top of that its own interpretation of what kind of maxima are significant.

In fact, it isn't even obvious to me what value I should use for `threshold_rel` if I don't know whether the input array can have negative values...

On a different issue, it is a bit strange that `exclude_border` always uses `min_distance`, even when `threshold` is given.  It would make much more sense to use `threshold` when available (in fact, it would be even better to merge the two arguments, `min_distance` and `threshold`, in a single one).

issue_title: skimage.transform.rescale does not support multidimensional scaling for multichannel images
issue_body:
## Description
When using an array for the scale argument of the rescale function an error is raised whenever the image being rescaled is multichannel. 

## Traceback
```
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
ValueError                                Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-61-27f3cec9c67e> in <module>()
----> 1 dat['img'].apply(rescale, scale=(.5, .5)).iloc[0].shape

~/anaconda3/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pandas/core/series.py in apply(self, func, convert_dtype, args, **kwds)
   2549             else:
   2550                 values = self.asobject
-> 2551                 mapped = lib.map_infer(values, f, convert=convert_dtype)
   2552 
   2553         if len(mapped) and isinstance(mapped[0], Series):

pandas/_libs/src/inference.pyx in pandas._libs.lib.map_infer()

~/anaconda3/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pandas/core/series.py in <lambda>(x)
   2536         # handle ufuncs and lambdas
   2537         if kwds or args and not isinstance(func, np.ufunc):
-> 2538             f = lambda x: func(x, *args, **kwds)
   2539         else:
   2540             f = func

[deleted]/skimage/transform/_warps.py in rescale(image, scale, order, mode, cval, clip, preserve_range, multichannel, anti_aliasing, anti_aliasing_sigma)
    267     if len(scale) > 1 and (len(scale) != image.ndim or
    268                            (multichannel and len(scale) != image.ndim - 1)):
--> 269         raise ValueError("must supply a single scale or one value per axis")
    270     orig_shape = np.asarray(image.shape)
    271     output_shape = np.round(scale * orig_shape)

ValueError: must supply a single scale or one value per axis
```

## What seems to be the cause
The problem seems to be this if statement:
```
if len(scale) > 1 and (len(scale) != image.ndim or (multichannel and len(scale) != image.ndim - 1)):
```
The statement after the first `and` always evaluates to True if `multichannel` is True. Seems like a simple fix, but there could be more bugs hiding under there.

issue_title: Loading indexed png file.

I have recently tried to an indexed png image in scikit-image, but didn't find a way to
make it using ```skimage.io``` ```imread```.

In matlab it can be done using:

[im, colormap] = imread('indexed.png');

Using PIL library:
```
from PIL import Image

im = Image.open("image.png") # Replace with your image name here
indexed = np.array(im) 
```

issue_title: Proposal: SP potentialRadius should default to -1
issue_body:
Proposal:

- In the SpatialPooler, support `potentialRadius=-1`. This value would disable any topology relationship between columns and inputs. This would save everyone from having to figure out their input width and setting `potentialRadius=inputWidth`.
- Change the default `potentialRadius` value from `16` to `-1`.
- Remove the [code in the SPRegion](https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/1f58cf0798fd5dc2626b44590e3dd8880bf601a1/src/nupic/regions/SPRegion.py#L467) which is already forcing everyone into this default. Respect people's model parameters -- allow them to set the `potentialRadius` when using the Network API.

Who this would break:

- Anybody using the raw SpatialPooler who is depending on the default value of `16`.

Alternate fixes:

- When `globalInhibition` is true, ignore the `potentialRadius`.
  - This would also work, but some people might want to experiment with using a potential radius with global inhibition. It adds some structure to the randomness.
  - This would be much more dramatic breaking change than changing the default value of `potentialRadius`.

Related links:

- Old issue: "SPRegion should respect potentialRadius model parameter" https://github.com/numenta/nupic/issues/839
- A blog post about the time I got bit by this: http://mrcslws.com/blocks/2016/03/31/column-sdr-not-random-enough.html
- This was also the culprit of a very long htm.java debugging spree: https://github.com/numenta/NAB/pull/237/commits/cc2855e4e113ca738dad789bf9273ccea6798c4e

issue_title: WebSearch: HTTP APIs to query available search fields/indexes, rankings, etc.
issue_body:
Originally on 2012-03-09

Along with the (soon-to-be) added HTTP APIs to retrieve the available collections of an Invenio-based repo, we could also add support for querying some additional information to launch a query via the search APIs. For eg, it should be possible to query about:

 - available fields/indexes (*author*, *title*, *language*, etc)
 - available ranking methods (*citation*, *wrd*, etc.)
 - available output formats (*xm*, *hb*, *hx*, etc.)
 - available languages (*en*, *it*, *de*, etc.)
 - etc. (?)

The returned structured (json?) should possibly contain a description for each value.

The above values could also be returned as part of the information sent along with the available collection list, as these values might also vary based on the collection. Alternatively, we could offer the possibility to filter the retrieved values per collection. For eg. the Invenio connector (client) could be augmented with the following functions:

```
def get_fields(c=None, ln=None):
   """
   Returns list of available search index, and their mapping to MARC fields:
   {'title': ['titolo', ['245__a', '246__b', '111__a']],
    'abstract': ['riassunto', ['520__%', '590__%']],
    ...
   }
   @param c: list of fields specifically relevant for given collection
   @param ln: language for the description. If not specified, default language 
              of the repository
   """
   [...]


def get_ranking_methods(c=None, ln=None):
   """
   Returns list of available ranking methods:
   {'wrd': 'word similarity',
    'citation': ''times cited',
    ...
   }
   @param c: list of rankings specifically relevant for given collection
   @param ln: language for the description. If not specified, default language 
              of the repository
   """
   [...]

def get_output_formats(c=None, ln=None):
   """
   Returns list of available output formats:
   {'hb': 'HTML brief',
    'hx': ''BibTeX',
    ...
   }
   @param c: list of output formats specifically relevant for given collection
   @param ln: language for the description. If not specified, default language 
              of the repository
   """
   [...]

def get_languages():
   """
   Returns list of available output languages
   {'en': 'English',
    'de': 'Deutsch',
    'it': 'Italiano',
    ...
   }
   """
   [...]
```

In the above examples one could imagine removing the *ln* parameter and returning all variants in the same call, to minimize number of requests.

It could also be made mandatory to authenticate to use these API, in order to invite developers to use these APIs with care (eg. with client-side caching).

issue_title: global: session activity feature
issue_body:
We need the ability to destroy a valid session without the user making a request (this is useful to e.g. ban users immediately #44). Also, this can be used to allow users to see a list of their active sessions (e.g. from multiple devices, and logout a specific device). This is only really possible when sessions are stored server-side (i.e #51). We also need to associate a session (for a logged in user) with a user id (Flask-Session/KVSession use uuids as session ids).

- [ ] Model: user, session, timestamp (UTC), country, browser, os (deduced from ip and user agent)
- [ ] Connect to Flask-Login's ``user_logged_in`` signal to store the session activity when a user logins.
- [ ] Admin interface (needs very restrictive permissions). Browse, remove, filter by user. (related to #33)
- [ ] Celery cron task to clean up session activity table.
- [ ] Configuration: Allow 1) tracking only user/session/timestamp and not country, browser and os 2) completely switching off the session activity feature.
- [ ] Account settings item "Security", with a panel that list Sessions (see e.g. DropBox as an example). Allow a user to remove a session plus "Log me out of all devices". I would also include "Change password" on the same page.

issue_title: Add config parameter for request timeout
issue_body:
We use `requests` internally to make calls out to the G API. By default, requests has no timeout defined, and it's up to the backend source to define when it'll give up. Currently we have a backend time out of 60 seconds, primarily due to resources like the bundler.

Requests greatly encourages we set a reasonable timeout and use it in every production call [1]

> You can tell Requests to stop waiting for a response after a given number of seconds with the timeout parameter. Nearly all production code should use this parameter in nearly all requests. Failure to do so can cause your program to hang indefinitely:

[1] http://docs.python-requests.org/en/master/user/quickstart/#timeouts

This should be added as a configurable parameter, with a reasonable default (20 or 30 seconds seems more than enough)

issue_title: Add support for jti claim
issue_body:
Hi all,

I'd like to add the support for jti claim to the package. The idea basically would be to store a list of expired jti's somewhere on the server side, and allow to blacklist specific tokens.

The way I'm thinking about the implementation is:

- Use pymongo to store the blacklist (rather than a django specific mongo package, makes our solution portable to vanilla python or other frameworks)
- Generate a long enough jti claim that will have negligible probability of being produced twice before the first one expired (string of 20 random ascii_letters and digits maybe?)
- Store the jti and the entire payload in mongodb, this will help us clean up the collection when a JWT is past the expiry date (so we don't grow the collection ad infinitum)
- Add a flag in api_settings.JWT_ENABLE_BLACKLIST, if True we add the jti claim to the payload in jwt_payload_handler()
- Check against the mongodb collection everytime we decode a JWT (if JWT_ENABLE_BLACKLIST is True) and if the jti is blacklisted treat it as if the JWT is expired

I have made an initial commit to a fork of the package, with my suggestion on how to implement this, you can find it here: https://github.com/avimeir/django-rest-framework-jwt/commits/master

Happy to discuss this!

Cheers,
Avi

issue_title: Token verification view
issue_body:
We are thinking of using this library to create an authentication microservice in a cluster of server-side applications.The current implementation is great for when your protected resources are on the same server as your JWT endpoint, but would need some alteration for if these were on separate apps.

The authentication flow I am considering is as follows:  a user would log on from our client-side single page app by hitting an endpoint on the auth microservice (AuthService) that serves the `obtain_jwt_token` view. They would then store their JWT in-browser. If the client-side application needed to request a resource from another server-side application (ResourceService), it would pass the JWT in the header. However, the idea is that ResourceService would not have to verify the authenticity of the JWT itself - rather it would make a secondary HTTP call to AuthService to check if the JWT was valid.

This would mean setting up a second type of view on the AuthService which received a JWT and verified its authenticity. I don't think this would be difficult to do by abstracting out most of the `authenticate` method of the `JSONWebTokenAuthentication` class to give it the flexibility to be used either as a function in a view or as a REST framework authentication class.

I wondered if you think a view like this would be a useful addition to the library, or if it is outside the scope of what you are wanting to achieve?

from kivy.app import App
form kivy.uix.label import Label

class MyApp(App):
  def build(self):
    return Label(text='test')
MyApp().run()

It runs on my computer, starting it with "python3 main.py". I can build an android apk with buildozer, but on my phone it crashes on startup. Logcat says "Import error, no module named request". I'm using the following:
Python 3.4.0
Buildozer 0.26
Kivy v1.8.0
running on Ubuntu 14.04.2
Any idea how to fix this?

issue_title: Scipy recipe
issue_body:
This is a request for a scipy recipe. 

Turns out scipy has a few more dependencies than numpy:

- BLAS: Can probably use https://github.com/xianyi/OpenBLAS/wiki/How-to-build-OpenBLAS-for-Android

- LAPACK: This one is tough; no fortran compiler in the NDK.  Can probably use this: https://github.com/simonlynen/android_libs/tree/master/lapack (clapack for android)

I'm still researching what else is needed.

```

The oddest thing is that while 
```
 ./distribute.sh -m "openssl pil kivy"
```
worked when I set up p4a fresh, once I have added zope2 into the build every time I try to build p4a no matter what i include it always fails with the above


To get this point (and thus replicate the bug/issue) I had to do the following:

- Use a 32bit Build environment in this case Ubutu 13.10
- Use this recipe to build Zope2:
```
#!/bin/bash

VERSION_zope2=${VERSION_zope2:-2.13}
URL_zope2=http://github.com/zopefoundation/Zope/archive/$VERSION_zope2.zip
BUILD_zope2=$BUILD_PATH/zope2/$(get_directory $URL_zope2)
RECIPE_zope2=$RECIPES_PATH/zope2

function prebuild_zope2() {
    true
}

function build_zope2() {
    cd $BUILD_zope2

    push_arm

    export LDFLAGS="$LDFLAGS -L$LIBS_PATH"
    export LDSHARED="$LIBLINK"

    HOSTPYTHON=/home/zac/Documents/Projects/Apps/python-for-android/build/hostpython/Python-2.7.2/hostpython
    try $HOSTPYTHON bootstrap.py
    try bin/buildout
        
    unset LDSHARED
    pop_arm
}

function postbuild_zope2() {
    true
}
```

- Fix a bug with Zope2 and _SSL by doing:
```
python-for-android/build/hostpython/Python-2.7.2/Lib ln -s ../build/lib.linux-x86_64-2.7 plat-linux3
```

And then everything should build to the point above

issue_title: Make docs processor more configurable
issue_body:
To use markdown in my api docs, right now I use monkey patching like this:

```python
import flask_swagger
import CommonMark

def sanitize(text):
    parser = CommonMark.DocParser()
    renderer = CommonMark.HTMLRenderer()
    ast = parser.parse(text)
    return renderer.render(ast)

flask_swagger._sanitize = sanitize
```

But this could be made a configurable parameter like:

```python
def swagger(app, process_doc=_sanitize):
    ...
```

issue_title: Limit member exposition from an instance
issue_body:
Hi,

firstly I would like to thank you guys for this great library.

I would like to know if there is a way for limiting the members I can return to Fire in my functions. Let me give you an example about how I (half) resolved this:

```
import fire

class Obj:
    def __init__(self):
        self.safe = 'safe!'

    def dangerous(self):
        print('dangerous!')

class App:
    def show(self):
        obj = Obj()
        return {'safe': obj.safe}

if __name__ == '__main__':
    fire.Fire(App, name='app')
```

This makes the job as I expect, although the `--help` functionality is not as friendly as I would need since, without any parameter, `show` returns the dictionary (as one could expect):

```
$ python app.py show 
safe: safe!
```
But, instead, I would need to have a behaviour similar to the following:

```
$ python app.py show --help
Usage:       app show 
             app show safe
```

Is it possible to enforce to show the help output if no parameter is provided to `show` instead of evaluating all it? Maybe using a dictionary for restricting the access to some members is not the right way to do it. I have also tried to return a `fire.Fire` instance but then I lose the execution chain in the usage (i.e. `app show ...`).

Thanks in advance.

issue_title: Support filter + groupby in a single expression
issue_body:
```
>>> import datatable as dt
>>> from datatable import f, sum
>>> DT = dt.Frame({"KEY": [1, 2, 1, 2, 1, 2], "X": [-10, 2, 3, 0, 1, -7]})
>>> DT
     KEY    X
---  ---  ---
 0     1  -10
 1     2    2
 2     1    3
 3     2    0
 4     1    1
 5     2   -7

[6 rows x 2 columns]

>>> DT[f.X > 0, sum(f.X), f.KEY]
     KEY  V0
---  ---  --
 0     1  -6
 1     2  -5

[2 rows x 2 columns]

>>> DT[:, sum(f.X), f.KEY]
     KEY  V0
---  ---  --
 0     1  -6
 1     2  -5

[2 rows x 2 columns]
```

In `DT[f.X > 0, sum(f.X), f.KEY]`, the filter `f.X > 0` should have left only the positive values of `X` for the summation, but in fact the result is exactly the same as if no filter was applied.

issue_title: test_wfirst.py currently fails
issue_body:
Running test_wfirst.py from the command line to get the extra, slightly more time-consuming tests is currently failing:
```
$ python test_wfirst.py
time for test_wfirst_wcs = 1.04
time for test_wfirst_backgrounds = 0.08
time for test_wfirst_bandpass = 0.06
time for test_wfirst_detectors = 0.02
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test_wfirst.py", line 519, in <module>
    test_wfirst_psfs()
  File "test_wfirst.py", line 468, in test_wfirst_psfs
    filename=test_file)
  File "/Library/Python/2.7/site-packages/galsim/wfirst/wfirst_psfs.py", line 319, in storePSFImages
    method='no_pixel')
  File "/Library/Python/2.7/site-packages/galsim/chromatic.py", line 2003, in drawImage
    wmult, integrator, self.gsparams)
  File "/Library/Python/2.7/site-packages/galsim/utilities.py", line 932, in __call__
    result = self.user_function(*key)
  File "/Library/Python/2.7/site-packages/galsim/chromatic.py", line 1805, in _get_effective_prof
    method='no_pixel')
  File "/Library/Python/2.7/site-packages/galsim/chromatic.py", line 1002, in drawImage
    int_im = self._get_interp_image(bandpass, image=image, integrator=integrator, **kwargs)
  File "/Library/Python/2.7/site-packages/galsim/chromatic.py", line 930, in _get_interp_image
    np.max(self.waves)))
RuntimeError: Requested wavelength 300.0 is outside the allowed range: 900.0 to 1230.0 nm
```
It looks like this is related to the changes in the default thinning/truncation stuff.  The 900-1230 range was designed to match the wavelength range for Y106, but now that filter goes from 300-2200, although most of those values are zeros.

The wfirst `getBandpasses` now has a kwarg `default_thin_trunc` which defaults to False, but given what this bandpass looks like, it seems like either that should probably default to True or we should store versions of the bandpasses that remove the extraneous zeros.

However, even if I do switch that to True, then it's still not quite right.  I need to change the 900 -> 885 on line [433 of test_wfirst.py](https://github.com/GalSim-developers/GalSim/blob/master/tests/test_wfirst.py#L433).  

And then the test still doesn't pass, but I'm not sure if this is a real problem or not:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test_wfirst.py", line 519, in <module>
    test_wfirst_psfs()
  File "test_wfirst.py", line 453, in test_wfirst_psfs
    err_msg='PSF at a given wavelength and interpolated chromatic one evaluated at that '

    precision=decimal)
  File "/Users/Mike/lib/python2.7/site-packages/numpy/testing/utils.py", line 713, in assert_array_compare
    raise AssertionError(msg)
AssertionError: 
Arrays are not almost equal to 3 decimals
PSF at a given wavelength and interpolated chromatic one evaluated at that wavelength disagree.
(mismatch 0.00629881582263%)
 x: array([[  1.295e-06,   3.367e-06,   3.891e-06, ...,   4.184e-06,
          3.012e-06,   1.152e-06],
       [  2.681e-06,   1.978e-06,   1.822e-06, ...,   1.622e-06,...
 y: array([[  7.226e-07,   1.001e-06,   2.299e-06, ...,   2.268e-06,
          8.914e-07,   7.136e-07],
       [  6.530e-07,   8.959e-07,   2.281e-06, ...,   2.115e-06,...
```
It's only 0.006% of the pixels, so my guess is this is just a numerical fluctuation, not an error.

issue_title: Annoying 'error: [Errno 32] Broken pipe' errors in patroni logs
issue_body:
I've got a lot of errors from Patroni when using with HAProxy:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/SocketServer.py", line 596, in process_request_thread
    self.finish_request(request, client_address)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/SocketServer.py", line 331, in finish_request
    self.RequestHandlerClass(request, client_address, self)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/SocketServer.py", line 654, in __init__
    self.finish()
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/SocketServer.py", line 713, in finish
    self.wfile.close()
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/socket.py", line 283, in close
    self.flush()
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/socket.py", line 307, in flush
    self._sock.sendall(view[write_offset:write_offset+buffer_size])
error: [Errno 32] Broken pipe
```

Solution copied from:
Bug 923611 - Haproxy causing broken pipe errors in logs for python-2.7 & python-3.3 
https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=923611

issue_title: Hyperlink support
issue_body:
This feature request is for the gtk3-based branch.

`VTE` 0.49.1 (and `GNOME Terminal` 3.25.1) has just implemented a brand new and hopefully really cool feature: hyperlinks (that is, HTML-like anchors).

This is different from the regex-based autodetection of URLs that appear onscreen. This time the text that appears does not have to look like a URL, it can be anything, and the target URL is specified explicitly by the utility along with the displayed text, using a particular escape sequence.

In case you'd like to hook up to this new feature (I hope you do), here's what to do:

- Call `vte_terminal_set_allow_hyperlink()` to enable the feature, as it's disabled by default.

- Whenever you're interested in the target URL at a certain location (e.g. for Ctrl+click action, or for presenting the right-click menu), call `vte_terminal_hyperlink_check_event()`. This returns a newly allocated string containing the URI-encoded URL that you should eventually free.

- If you'd like to stay up to date with the URL as the mouse moves or the contents underneath change, hook up to the `hyperlink-hover-uri-changed` signal. It'll give you two parameters owned by VTE (that is, must not be modified or freed, and only valid for the duration of the signal handler). The first is the URI-encoded URL, the second is a bounding rectangle of the anchor text (relative to GtkWindow's topleft). [GNOME Terminal shows a tooltip with the URI-decoded and un-IDN'd URL (this decoding is done by GNOME Terminal, not by VTE). It's up to you to choose different presentations if you wish, e.g. an overlay in the bottom corner similarly to browsers, or whatever you prefer. There's even a pending demo patch for GNOME Terminal to show a preview for certain local files (most notably pictures, PDFs).]

When handling `file:` URLs, please double check that you verify the hostname, as described in [1]. This is unfortunately not done by `gtk_show_uri()` or its underlying `g_app_info_launch_default_for_uri()`.

In case a character cell belongs to both an explicit URL (this new feature) and an implicit autodetected one (the old one, using regex match), we recommend the explicit one to take precedence (we assume that the application emitting the URL knows it much better than the autodetecting regex). This is also how VTE underlines the cells, so this is what the user expects on e.g. a Ctrl+click. The right-click menu might display both entries, in GNOME Terminal we decided to display actions for the explicit hyperlink only in case of such collision.

Keep in mind that we're in a development cycle, there's a slim chance that the API will change before it's finalized in VTE 0.50 this September. I'll let you know here if it changes.

For further information on this feature, see

[1] A dedicated page with all the details: https://gist.github.com/egmontkob/eb114294efbcd5adb1944c9f3cb5feda

[2] The GNOME Terminal bugreport (way too long and probably not that interesting at this point): https://bugzilla.gnome.org/show_bug.cgi?id=779734

issue_title: Track API requests with Piwik
issue_body:
On each API call, send a “pageview” to a Piwik instance. This will allow for usage analytics.
This tracking must be nonblocking and have only a minimal impact on performance. A library such as [`grequests`](https://github.com/kennethreitz/grequests) should be used on [Piwik HTTP API](http://developer.piwik.org/api-reference/tracking-api), or a specific wrapper such as [`piwikapi`](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/piwikapi).

The API must still work if the tracker is not installed.

- [ ] Create a `openfisca/tracker` package.
- [ ] Send a “pageview” on each API call, with the URL being the called endpoint URL.
- [ ] Measure performance impact: benchmark the time to handle 100 computations, 100 parameters requests, before and after the change, in 3 different runs.
- [ ] Document that behaviour, explain why this is done, explain why there are no CNIL & EU privacy requirements in the README of that package.
- [ ] Add a target URL setup to opt-in to tracking (`TRACKER=https://stats.openfisca.org`).
- [ ] Add an information line in the doc.
- [ ] Output a reassuring information message on API start if tracking is set up.

    • ``'^'`` – bitwise AND (*only for 32-bit integers*)

issue_title: Python API: Parent ParameterCollection declared locally is destroyed at runtime
issue_body:
Hi, after quite a struggle I found Python runtime would destroy parent `ParameterCollection` once it's out of scope, causing all child `ParameterCollection`s unusable.

The story is, I have a Python object of `Parser(args, parent_model)`,  whose constructor looks like:
```
def __init__(self, args, parent_model):
    if parent_model:
        self.model = parent_model.add_subcollection('/parser')
    else:
        _model = dy.ParameterCollection()
        self.model = _model.add_subcollection('/parser')
```

During training, `Parser` is optimized together with other components (with their own `ParameterCollection`s), so I use `parent_model` to store all parameters to optimize, while at test time I just need the `Parser` object, without `parent_model`.

However, the above code will cause segmentation fault when `parent_model=None`. This is because (my hypothesis) `_model` is destroyed by runtime after the `__init__()` call, causing all subcollections to be destroyed. This shouldn't have happened since I assume the subcollection `/parser` will hold a reference to the temporary `_model` object, and in this case `_model` will not be destroyed. However, this happens only in C++ and the Python object `ParameterCollection` does not keep track of its parent/child parameter collections.

issue_title: File structure refactoring
issue_body:
Currently compiling DyNet takes a long time for a couple of reasons:
* Inclusion of header files is not clean, and header files are included when not necessary in a number of places.
* `nodes.cc` is a super-huge file that implements virtually all operations.

I think we should refactor the file structure so that

- [x] `nodes.cc` is split into a finer-grained divisions, perhaps similar to the doc: http://dynet.readthedocs.io/en/latest/operations.html
- [x] `nodes-common.cc` is removed, with all the functionality being moved to the appropriate files from the previous bullet point. This will make sure that the code implementing a particular node will all be in the same file.
- [ ] All header file includes that are not necessary are removed.
- [ ] Other files that have lots of different classes implemented (e.g. `dynet.h` have the functionality split into different files named after the classes so that they're easier to find (e.g. `computation-graph.h` and `node.h`)

issue_title: issue with _interpolate_azimuth_edges
issue_body:
Hi,

I encountered a very weird issue with the function _half_angle_complex in transforms.py.

If the azimuth angles of the rays are all the same, due to rounding the dot_product may be larger than one and therefore produce a nan value. In the newest versions of matplotlib pcolormesh cannot handle masked values for the x or y arguments and therefore it raises an exception.

I solved the issue simply imposing that the dot_product cannot be larger than one:

def _half_angle_complex(complex_angle1, complex_angle2):
    """
    Return half the angle between complex numbers on the unit circle.

    Parameters
    ----------
    complex_angle1, complex_angle2 : complex
        Complex numbers representing unit vectors on the unit circle

    Returns
    -------
    half_angle : float
        Half the angle between the unit vectors in degrees.

    """
    dot_product = np.real(complex_angle1 * np.conj(complex_angle2))
    if dot_product > 1:
        warnings.warn('dot_product larger than one')
        dot_product = 1.
    full_angle_rad = np.arccos(dot_product)
    half_angle_rad = full_angle_rad / 2.
    half_angle_deg = np.rad2deg(half_angle_rad)
    return half_angle_deg

You can test the issue by checking what happen with angle 274. with regular float precision.

I hope I am clear.

issue_title: dns.Client.zone requires more arguments than just the name.
issue_body: Notably `Client.zone` requires `name` and `dns_name`, but should only require `name`.

issue_title: Make Bucket/Blob/_PropertyMixin have "future" functionality
issue_body:
This is relating to a discussion @tseaver and I just had about enabling `GET` in batches.

Something like

```python
with storage.Batch():
    bucket = storage.get_bucket('foo')
    print bucket.cors
    blob = storage.Blob('bar', bucket=bucket, eager=True)  # See #761 about eager
```

is not possible because the actual `GET` which retrieves the `bucket` happens on the `__exit__` of the batch.

-----

We could allow `_PropertyMixin` objects to act as a future by setting a property (e.g. `bucket._is_future = True`) and then making getters and setters fail when `_is_future == True`.

issue_title: Check query's run status using property, not attribute
issue_body:
Split out from #3001, where @amirshavit reported:

> The run method checks if `self._job is not None`: to prevent a double invocation. It should however run that check with `self.job` instead (no underscore), as `self._job` is only set if you previously used `self.job`.

  Total messages sent: 66640
  ```

- Initialization
  - GCP project `GOOGLE_CLOUD_PROJECT`
  - Pub/Sub API enabled
  - Existing topic `PUBSUB_TOPIC`
  - Environment variables `GOOGLE_CLOUD_PROJECT` & `PUBSUB_TOPIC`

- Subscriber sample code `<pubsub_subscribe.py>`
  ```python
  import os
  from socket import gethostname
  from time import sleep
  from google.cloud import pubsub
  from google.api_core.exceptions import AlreadyExists

  PROJECT_ID = os.getenv('GOOGLE_CLOUD_PROJECT')
  TOPIC = os.getenv('PUBSUB_TOPIC')

  def on_pubsub_message(msg):
      # These cases are not handled:
      # - A message may be received more than once
      # - A message may be received out of order
      cmd = msg.data
      msg.ack()
      # print('.. msg: %s' % cmd)

      global msg_received_total
      global msg_received_loop
      global subscription

      if cmd == 'stop':
          print("Received 'stop'")
          msg_received_total += msg_received_loop
          subscription.future.set_result(True)
          return

      msg_received_loop += 1
      if cmd == 'loop_end':
          print('Messages received: %d + %d' % (
              msg_received_total, msg_received_loop))
          msg_received_total += msg_received_loop
          msg_received_loop = 0

  msg_received_total = 0
  msg_received_loop = 0

  subscriber = pubsub.SubscriberClient()
  topic_path = subscriber.topic_path(PROJECT_ID, TOPIC)
  subsc_path = subscriber.subscription_path(PROJECT_ID, gethostname())

  try:
      subscriber.create_subscription(subsc_path, topic_path)
  except AlreadyExists:
      pass

  subscription = subscriber.subscribe(subsc_path, on_pubsub_message)
  print('Subscribed to messages on %s' % subsc_path)

  print('Waiting for future...')
  subscription.future.result()

  print('Total messages received: %d' % msg_received_total)
  ```

- Publisher sample code `<pubsub_publish.py>`
  ```python
  import os
  from time import sleep
  from google.cloud import pubsub

  PROJECT_ID = os.getenv('GOOGLE_CLOUD_PROJECT')
  TOPIC = os.getenv('PUBSUB_TOPIC')

  PARAMS = [
      # (number_of_messages, seconds_after_each_message)
      (300, 1),  # 5 min
      (3000, 0.1),  # 5 min
      (30000, 0.01),  # 5 min
      (30, 10),  # 5 min
      (5, 60),  # 5 min
      (3, 600),  # 30 min
      (2, 3600),  # 120 min
      (30000, 0.01),  # 5 min
      (3000, 0.1),  # 5 min
      (300, 1),  # 5 min
  ]

  publisher = pubsub.PublisherClient()
  topic_path = publisher.topic_path(PROJECT_ID, TOPIC)

  msg_sent = 0
  for messages, delay in PARAMS:
      for i in xrange(1, messages):
          publisher.publish(topic_path, 'msg#{}'.format(i).encode('utf-8'))
          sleep(delay)
      # Keep last message to send a 'loop_end' command
      sleep(1)
      publisher.publish(topic_path, b'loop_end')
      sleep(1)
      print('Messages sent: %d + %d' % (msg_sent, messages))
      msg_sent += messages

  # publisher.publish(topic_path, b'stop')
  print('Total messages sent: %d' % msg_sent)

Also note this is still affected by: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/issues/4426

```python

def list_topics(project):
    """Lists all Pub/Sub topics in the given project."""
    logging.debug(f"Retrieving topics for project: {project}")
    project_path = publisher.project_path(project)
    return publisher.list_topics(project_path)

def list_subscriptions_in_topic(topic_path):
    """Lists all subscriptions for a given topic."""
    logging.debug(f"Retrieving subscriptions for topic: {topic_path}")
    return subscriber.list_subscriptions(topic_path)


def get_existing(project):
    # retrieve existing topics and subscriptions to compare against
    # this will return the path for each
    # this list comprehension extracts just the topic name from the list of topic objects
    logging.info(f"Retrieving existing topics and subscriptions for project {project}")
    existing_topics = [topic.name for topic in list_topics(project)]
    existing_subscriptions = []
    for e_topic in existing_topics:
        # this list comprehension extracts just the subscription name from the list of subscription objects
        existing_subscriptions.extend([subscription.name for subscription in list_subscriptions_in_topic(e_topic)])
    # currently the library lists all subscriptions not just for that topic
    # so I call set on the the end list to remove duplicates.
    # This should be forward compatible once Google fixes the library
    existing_subscriptions = set(existing_subscriptions)
    return existing_topics, existing_subscriptions

```

issue_title: feature request: query without having to provide a jobid ?
issue_body:
i'm reading the `google-cloud-python` docs and the bigquery rest api reference [2], the job insert api is the async query job, I wonder why the python api is designed to have to provide a jobid? since bigquery server side is archiving the job id for a long time can't re-use same id, that has imposed client side a burden to maintain every jobid to be unique, although that can be easily archived by a time and random based id generator, but the point is: if the bottom layer rest api reference [2] doesn't require jobid to be provided, in the case when jobid is omitted, bigquery server will allocate a unique jobid; why the Python api becomes requiring a jobid?

1. https://googlecloudplatform.github.io/google-cloud-python/stable/bigquery-usage.html#querying-data-asynchronous
2. https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/reference/rest/v2/jobs

issue_title: better methods for start and end times/values
issue_body:
Maybe something like:
```
ti, tf = ts.get_first_and_last_times()

vi, vf = ts.get_first_and_last_values()

(ti, vi), (tf, vf) = ts.get_first_and_list()
```

to be better than:

```
ti, vi = ts.get_by_index(0)
tf, vf = ts.get_by_index(-1)
```

(or at least have `get_by_index` documented)

issue_title: KCPTUN 的UDP断流厉害，有什么TCP加速方案抗干扰的么？最好是单边加速的
issue_body:
前两天安装了KCPtun,好不容易安装成功，就连上了30分钟左右，占用宽带10-20mps左右，没有太离谱，结果就断流了。

稳定可用是前提啊，有其他TCP加速或者TCP单边方案么？ 最好可以配合SS使用的，谢谢各位了！！！

小白，静候大侠帮忙指点！

issue_title: shadowsocks 在公司内网无法使用
issue_body: 我在digital ocean运行了server 端的程序，在公司的windows 7上面装了windows客户端. 如果把公司电脑带回家，可以使用shadowsocks翻墙。但是在公司的内网时候，用相同的设定，浏览器就无法连接到网络。 客户端的log全部是 timeout的信息，但是server端的log没有显示有client尝试连接。我估计是客户端的信息被过滤掉了。我使用了其他proxy端口都失败了。我想问一下这个公司防火墙原理是什么？（如果我猜想是对的话）

Shadowsocks Server Running out of Memory
issue_body:
I tried to deploy a Shadowsocks server upon Ubuntu 11.04 version, on a VPS in US.  The server runs OK each time when it got boot up.  But my Shadowsocks clients stopped responding after a couple of days.  The only way I've figured out is to reboot the VM to make it right. 
    When I connected to the VM using ssh, I could see the server memory be almost used up.  Could it be an unreported memory leak issue?

issue_title: Disabling Rollback and Custom S3 Bucket/Key
issue_body:
I just have a couple questions for some things I haven't been able to find. I have dug around the source a bit but just wanted to verify here.

1. Is it possible to disable the rollback feature if the stack fails to deploy? We have a lot of stuff that goes on in UserData and if that fails, we like to get on the EC2 instance and troubleshoot. With rollback, it kills the instances so we can't do this anymore.

2. I am aware that you can set a custom bucket for where the template is uploaded, but is it possible to also set the Key so it goes into specific folders in the bucket?

issue_title: Add support for 'excludes' to PathGlobs
issue_body:
These are currently not supported in PathGlobs, but will need to be to allow us to parse all BUILD files in the pantsbuild/pants repo.

They are currently banned in a few places:
https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/f83e2b0272e2ff1c464e453c1f74d9335e18fcc3/src/python/pants/engine/exp/fs.py#L227 is one.
https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/f83e2b0272e2ff1c464e453c1f74d9335e18fcc3/src/python/pants/engine/exp/legacy/globs.py#L40-L41 is another, but it is more implicit. We drop them immediately.

----

The true test of whether this is working is whether:
```
./pants run src/python/pants/engine/exp/legacy:filemap -- src/::
```
succeeds and excludes, rather than failing with:
```
pants.build_graph.address_lookup_error.AddressLookupError: Build graph construction failed for DescendantAddresses(directory='src'):
  Failed to parse src/python/pants/reporting/BUILD:
kwargs not supported for <class 'pants.engine.exp.legacy.globs.Globs'>. Got: {'exclude': [['report.py']]}
```

issue_title: Create a 'recipies' documentation page
issue_body:
I mentioned this in a thread on pants-devel and in a discussion in https://rbcommons.com/s/twitter/r/1979/

I would like to propose a 'Recipes' documentation page where you mention how to use Pants to solve different problems.  Initially I would like to contribute "recipies" for:
-  developing with a local jar file, 
- using from_target() and unpacked_jars(), and 
- configuring a maven repo to live side by side with pants.  

This kind of document would be good not just for showing how to do things, but for expressing some opinions about the relative merits of these configurations too because they all seem to have their own controversies.

issue_title: Add `--transitive` flag for all linters
issue_body:
We should add a `--[no]-transitive` flag for all lint tasks to support the usecase where you're running directly on changed targets.

Running directly on changed targets happens when you use `./pants --changed lint` (for any setting of the `--changed-include-dependees` option): in that case, you know that only files directly owned by a target are affected. It also happens during `arc lint`.

Because this affects so many tasks, it seems likely that it should go on a (perhaps lint specific?) Subsystem and be consumed there.

issue_title: `pants filter` behavior & docs disagree
issue_body:
On SHA `ff9fdec36640d554e575762cc7a1d3ae6c5f4a7b`, I'm seeing some behavior with `./pants filter` that is surprising to me. Consider these two targets:

```
[prometheus pants (master)]$ grep _library examples/src/java/org/pantsbuild/example/hello/greet/BUILD examples/src/scala/org/pantsbuild/example/hello/welcome/BUILD 
examples/src/java/org/pantsbuild/example/hello/greet/BUILD:java_library(name = 'greet',
examples/src/scala/org/pantsbuild/example/hello/welcome/BUILD:scala_library(name='welcome',
```

I would think that these 3 commands would be equivalent:

```
[prometheus pants (master)]$ PANTS_DEV=1 ./pants filter --filter-type='+java_library' --filter-type='+scala_library' examples/src/java/org/pantsbuild/example/hello/greet examples/src/scala/org/pantsbuild/example/hello/welcome
examples/src/scala/org/pantsbuild/example/hello/welcome:welcome

[prometheus pants (master)]$ PANTS_DEV=1 ./pants filter --filter-type='+scala_library' --filter-type='+java_library' examples/src/java/org/pantsbuild/example/hello/greet examples/src/scala/org/pantsbuild/example/hello/welcome
examples/src/java/org/pantsbuild/example/hello/greet:greet

[prometheus pants (master)]$ PANTS_DEV=1 ./pants filter --filter-type='+scala_library,java_library' examples/src/java/org/pantsbuild/example/hello/greet examples/src/scala/org/pantsbuild/example/hello/welcome
examples/src/java/org/pantsbuild/example/hello/greet:greet
examples/src/scala/org/pantsbuild/example/hello/welcome:welcome
```

But they are not. The docs imply that this should be the case:

```
[prometheus pants (master)]$ PANTS_DEV=1 ./pants filter -h |grep -A 5 filter-type
  --filter-type=_FILTER_TYPE__
  --type _FILTER_TYPE__   Target types to include (optional '+' prefix) or
                          exclude ('-' prefix). Multiple type inclusions or
                          exclusions can be specified in a comma-separated list
                          or by using multiple instances of this flag. (default:
                          [])
```

Either the docs are wrong or pants is, I'm not sure which.

issue_title: Audit GO env vars and scrub more of them as appropriate
issue_body: Currently [GoDistribution](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/contrib/go/src/python/pants/contrib/go/subsystems/go_distribution.py) controls GOROOT and GOPATH but no others.  This could be dangerous.

issue_title: Python constants in BUILD files not defined in new engine
issue_body:
The [parser in use in the new engine](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/engine/legacy/parser.py) currently does not support variables defined in BUILD files.

A snippet like the following will cause an error indicating that the constant is an unknown symbol:
```
VERSION='1.0.0'

jar_library(name='foo', jars=[jar('org.bar', 'foo', VERSION)])
```

issue_title: Generated .ensime file is invalid
issue_body:
https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/backend/project_info/tasks/templates/ensime/ensime.mustache

Generates a file which does not include a `:sala-version` at the top level, which the Atom text editor's Ensime plugin appears to require (others, too, perhaps). Additionally, the format it expect is like `"2.11.8"` while the one the template generates is like `"Scala 2.11"`

issue_title: Subaddress parsing (Address Ranges)
issue_body:
I'm working with a dataset that contains a lot of address ranges.  I did a quick scan of the issues and didn't see anyone discussing these kinds of addresses.

For example:
````
>>> usaddress.tag('4120-4140 Annapolis Rd, Baltimore, MD 21227')
(OrderedDict([('AddressNumber', u'4120-4140'), ('StreetName', u'Annapolis'), ('StreetNamePostType', u'Rd'), ('PlaceName', u'Baltimore'), ('StateName', u'MD'), ('ZipCode', u'21227')]), 'Street Address')
````

In this case, per the URISA documentation, I might expect the Address Number to be some type of Subaddress, given one of their definitions of a "Complete Subaddress" is "Two Complete Address Numbers, separated by a hyphen, and followed by a Complete Street Name (in the case of a Two Number Address Range)".

Right now, I'm leaning towards usaddress' current behavior being the most correct and that I should handle the distinction myself, but I wanted to open the discussion.

issue_title: Labeller incompatible with Python 3
issue_body:
* The `label` function in `manual_labeling` expects the input to be Unicode-encoded, but Python 3 `open` defaults to Unicode.
* `data_prep_util`'s `appendListToXMLfile` doesn't specify a write mode (thus defaulting to Unicode), while `etree.tostring` produces a bytes string.
* `etree.tostring` will have XML-escaped Unicode characters, so decoding its output in [`test_manual_labeling`](https://github.com/datamade/parserator/blob/master/tests/test_manual_labeling.py) accomplishes nothing.

issue_title: Drop support for python 2.6
issue_body:
Quoted from the original description in #263

> From #255
> 
> The new versions of pylint and astroid have dropped support for Python 2.6 (and presumably also for python 3.2). At the moment, we have pinned to old versions in requirements-dev.txt. We will have to decide what to do about our own Python 2.6 support going forward. One option may be to have Travis use different versions of pylon etc for Py2.6, but this seems a bit messy.

For reference, please see the lengthy discussion about the issues pertaining to dropping support for Python 2.6 in #263.

issue_title: sqlalchemy MATCH support is not properly escaped
issue_body:
Adding a single quote to a query utilizing MATCH will break the query. I modified the test case to induce the behavior:

```
    def test_simple_match(self):
        query = self.session.query(self.Character.name) \
                    .filter(match(self.Character.name, 'Trill\'ian'))
        self.assertSQL(
            "SELECT characters.name AS characters_name FROM characters WHERE match(characters.name, 'Trill\\'ian')",
            query
        )
```

Result:

```
Failure
Expected :"SELECT characters.name AS characters_name FROM characters WHERE match(characters.name, 'Trill\\'ian')"
Actual   :"SELECT characters.name AS characters_name FROM characters WHERE match(characters.name, 'Trill'ian')"
```

The output of the test error is a little misleading - the comparison is of course taking place without the escapes.

issue_body: Two identical WARCs run through the indexer in a single command will generate two consecutive sets of CDXJ lines. De-duplication should be performed for the CDXJ content to be outputted prior to being outputted. This could be as simple as UNIQUEing exact duplicate lines.

issue_title: Change serve task in gulp to be the default task
Handling included layers as dependencies (build from branch/commit)
issue_body:
We should provide charm authors with the option to build against branches (and/or commits) of the layers they are using.

Included layers are a dependency to the charm and we should have an official way to handle this kind of dependency in the same way we for example handle pip packages.

By building charms against branches of layers (not only from the master head) we enable charm authors to follow a more conventional workflow. I am pleased to see the recent set of opened issues that work towards exposing details of a build making it reproducible.

Layer authors can
- have stable branches,
- be able to do backwards incompatible changes,
- perform hot fixes,
- protect downstream charms from experimental/buggy code and features

The charm authors can
- protect themselves from unstable layers,
- have reproducible build artefacts,
- allow for charm bug fixes without bringing in new experimental/buggy code

I am well aware of the good practice we try to impose by not supporting builds from layer branches. We essentially force authors to fix broken layers and by doing so we promote sharing of layers and operational knowledge. However, the lack of branch support will probably force charm authors to fork all the layers they are using for the reasons presented above. We should consider is that by convention the master head is the most recent code. As such it tends to be unstable and has more bugs than a stable branch. And any charm you build is as stable as the most unstable layer used. I think this hurts adoption.

Finally I would like to bring to your attention the golang dependency management paradigm [1] that we could could easily follow.

Thank you for your consideration.

[1] https://golang.org/cmd/go/#hdr-Vendor_Directories

```
(python2) ➜  codalab-cli git:(master) ✗ worker/codalabworker/worker.sh --server http://localhost:2900 --password ~/.codalab/root.password --tag s6

the output is

issue_title: Move girder_io and docker plugin code out of girder_worker.core
issue_body:
### Deliverables:
+ Maintain backward compatibility with girder_worker.run
+ Allow custom tasks to leverage girder_io and docker functionality.
### Technical Notes:
+ girder_io input/output type becomes a shim for library stored outside of girder_worker.core
+ docker exec and plugin become a shim for library stored outside of girder_worker.core
+ Not all tasks that want to do I/O with girder,  or run docker tasks will fit perfectly into the docker_run paradigm (e.g.  conditionally run one docker container over another).  Rather than overloading docker_run() with many many many options,  allow custom packages to implement their own task that uses the basic mechanics of docker and girder I/O. 
### Depends on: None

issue_title: Implement a simple blocking submit-poll-retrieve function
issue_body:
Sometimes, the whole parallelization scheme we have in place is an overkill, and all a user wants is to blockingly submit a problem and retrieve the result.

For this simple case we don't need thread pools and locks, making the `Client` trivially pickleable (#216, https://github.com/dwavesystems/dwave-system/issues/88).

issue_title: The output from ElmoEmbedder's embed_file conflicts with h5py naming convention
issue_body:
If I understand correctly, [```embed_file```](https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/master/allennlp/commands/elmo.py#L296) normally stores an h5py Dataset for each sentence in the input file. 
However, if the input sentence contains a forward slash, it will instead store an h5py **Group** object, collecting all sentences with a common prefix. 
See h5py documentation [here](https://support.hdfgroup.org/HDF5/doc1.6/UG/09_Groups.html).

For instance, embedding the following raw sentences will store one Group (containing the embeddings of the first two sentences) and one Dataset object (containing the embedding for the first): 

```
test / this is a first sentence
test / this is a second sentence
this is a third sentence
```

This is probably not the expected behavior?
Can escaping the forward slash character be a good solution?
So far, I couldn't find how to do that nicely in h5py.

@schmmd

issue_title: Slow write performance compared to v2.6
issue_body:
Run `%timeit` in IPython to measure performance of writing 6000 points in both influxdb-python v2.6 and v2.7.3:

```python
import numpy as np
from pandas import DataFrame, date_range
rng = date_range('2015-1-1', periods=6000, freq='s')
data = DataFrame({'value': np.random.randn(6000)}, index=rng)
measurement = 'perf_test'
tags = {"a": "val-a", "b": "val-b", "c": "val-c"}
%timeit -n3 -r10 dfclient.write_points(data, measurement, tags=tags, time_precision='u', batch_size=500)
```

Performance in v2.6:
```3 loops, best of 10: 390 ms per loop```

Performance in v2.7.3:
```3 loops, best of 10: 1.02 s per loop```

It is significantly slower.

issue_title: Add native (list etc) sources support
issue_body:
# Overview

Sometimes we want use only processors avoid loading and parsing (for example bigquery/sql integrations can be improved to support dict data in `import_resource` function without boilerplate code after feature will be implemented).

# Analysis

```python
print('\nUsage of native lists:')
source = [['id', 'name'], ['1', 'english'], ('2', '中国人')]
with topen(source, with_headers=True) as table:
    print(table.headers)
    for row in table:
        print(row)


print('\nUsage of native lists (keyed):')
source = [{'id': '1', 'name': 'english'}, {'id': '2', 'name': '中国人'}]
with topen(source, with_headers=True) as table:
    print(table.headers)
    for row in table:
        print(row)
```

# Tasks

- [x] add `loaders.API.source` to pass thru loader
- [x] implement `NativeLoader`
- [x] implement `NativeParser`
- [x] add it to `topen`
- [x] write tests and examples
- [x] find a way how to process not only list also generators and iterators

issue_title: Allowing Multi-threaded LpVariable construction
issue_body:
Hi,

I am currently working on a business problem that requires a thorough graph exploration to build relevant LpVariable for pulp problem.
As the size of the problem grew, this step was taking longer and longer and we decided to go parallel as we have naturally independant variables sets (typically a dozen or so, hence as many as CPU cores on our computers). Parallelism was implemented using multiprocessing.Pool under Python 2.7.

Unfortunately, I experienced some random crash **after** having runned pulp: Graph exploration was running fine (and multiprocessing exited properly) but I faced stackoverflows here and then when building my MIP problem later on: 
* sometimes when building constraint A
* sometimes when building constraint B 
* or even when defining problem's objective function...

Eventually I found out that my issue stem from the **hash function** of LpElement: it is defined from the id() function that returns the memory address holding the LPVariable. 
When runned in parallel, it might happened that different variables will hold the same hash value. Later on, if these variables were added to the same constraint or objective function, LPAffineExpression's internal dict had to rely on LpElement::__eq__ operator to store them properly, which in turn caused the StackOverflow on LpAffineExpression having a few hundreds of items.

I propose a pull request that solves that issue for me, but I guess familiar pulp developpers may consider other way of fixing it. As long as this can go in the master branch someday and help other people run pulp variable creation in paralle, I will be glad :)

Cheers,

Julien

issue_title: Proposal: Deploy docker images from private registries.
issue_body:
It would be great to be able to deploy pre-built docker images from private registries. As an idea each app could be configured using the `deis` client with a set of params to identify the registry endpoint, username, and password. I would suggest doing it at the app level as it gives the flexibility to have different images coming from different private registries. Something like the following to manage the registries:

```
deis registry:add
deis registry:list
deis registry:remove
```

On adding a registry the user would be prompted for that registries `endpoint`, `username`, and `password` which would then be stored internally.

Each app could then be told to get it's images from one of the stored registries. Perhaps setting the app level registry like so:

```
deis apps:registry -a <whatever> --registry=<name>
```

Could then use the `deis pull` command to make the image deployment.

issue_title: Deis integration with Kubernetes
issue_body:
I have a kubernetes cluster of CoreOS nodes running on AWS, and I'm now looking into how to setup a Continuous Integration & Continuous Deployment workflow on top of it.
The goal is to do something like that:

1. git commit on specific branch (or git tag)
2. unit tests checked
3. docker build & push to docker private registry node
4. kubectl create "test" pod
5. integration/acceptance tests checked
6. kubecfg rollingUpdate "production" replicationController

@gabrtv wrote on [StackOverflow](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/27242980/whats-the-difference-between-kubernetes-flynn-deis#comment43038384_27280549):

     Deis has already begun prototyping container orchestration via Kubernetes

Can you share some resources for me to test that prototype and help with its integration?

@kernel8liang you apparently tried to run Deis with Kubernetes in #1741, but it seems that you used fleet directly, bypassing kubernetes, hence they shouldn't be registered as pods in kubernetes. Did you make any more progress?

----

Here is how I believe the integration should be done (without reading the Deis doc yet):

* Create a specific aws autoscaling group, where all kubernetes minions/nodes will have deis autoinstalled with cloud-config
* Start all deis services with systemd on CoreOS or as fleetctl units
* Replace fleetctl calls into kubectl calls in Deis
* OR keep the fleetctl calls, but follow them with kube-register calls to notify kubernetes about those new pods.

issue_title: Creating a client with `environment="production"` does not properly translate as it would with the environment variable
issue_body:
The default config section has the "production" environment values.
We have handling code for this in `globus_sdk.config_get_default_environ`, but that only runs if `environment` isn't passed to a client.
As a result, config lookups are done against the `production` section and fail.

There are several ways of handling this, but one option which is probably pretty foolproof is to update this:
https://github.com/globus/globus-sdk-python/blob/d761ba9a104ba2d8a70d2e064d12ea95101596df/globus_sdk/base.py#L85-L86

Instead of doing that, try
```python
environment = config.get_default_environ(environment)
```
and update `get_default_environ` to take an input.

issue_title: Expose underlying API error data more directly / easily
issue_body:
Right now, each `GlobusAPIError` contains an `_underlying_response` object.
If you want to catch API errors and do something with the full JSON or text document of the error without subclassing `GlobusAPIError`, you have to manually pull out the response object and inspect it.

Because our error handling can sometimes be incomplete in the fields it presents, and especially because complex document types are sometimes embedded in errors, I'd like us to expose this error information more directly.

Can we add `GlobusAPIError.raw_json` and `GlobusAPIError.raw_text` as direct ways of accessing the underlying parsed JSON and text body of the response?
I have a use-case for this that isn't against Auth or Transfer as I'm prototyping things, but it also seems like it would be useful for the more complex errors exposed by Auth.

issue_title: DMRS links pointing to quantifiers rather than head nouns
issue_body:
In DMRS, predicates are collapsed with their ARG0 variables as a single node.  Whenever another predicate takes that variable as an argument, there should be a DMRS link to that node.  However, when loading from simplemrs and dumping to dmrx, the link may point to the quantifier node instead.

Minimal example:

`>>> mrs = delphin.mrs.simplemrs.loads_one('[ RELS: < [ _the_q LBL: h3 ARG0: x1 RSTR: h2 ] [ _dog_n_1 LBL: h1 ARG0: x1 ] [ _bark_v_1 LBL: h4 ARG0: e1 ARG1: x1 ] > HCONS: < h2 qeq h1 > ]')`
`>>> delphin.mrs.dmrx.dumps_one(mrs)`
`'<dmrs-list><dmrs cfrom="-1" cto="-1"><node cfrom="-1" cto="-1" nodeid="10000"><realpred lemma="_the_q" /><sortinfo cvarsort="x" /></node><node cfrom="-1" cto="-1" nodeid="10001"><realpred lemma="_dog_n_1" /><sortinfo cvarsort="x" /></node><node cfrom="-1" cto="-1" nodeid="10002"><realpred lemma="_bark_v_1" /><sortinfo cvarsort="e" /></node><link from="10000" to="10001"><rargname>RSTR</rargname><post>H</post></link><link from="10002" to="10000"><rargname>ARG1</rargname><post>NEQ</post></link></dmrs></dmrs-list>'`

The last link is from 10002 to 10000, but it should be to 10001 instead.

issue_title: activate breaks on unbound vars in some bash shells
issue_body:
The deactivate script [attempts to access $CONDA_PATH_BACKUP](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/402ace6f77775f2d7965128a0f7f42243142f32c/shell/deactivate#L55) even when it isn't bound (for example, [when `activate` sources `deactivate`](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/402ace6f77775f2d7965128a0f7f42243142f32c/shell/activate#L59)). This makes `deactivate` exit with an error message when sourced from a bash shell with [the `nounset` option](http://tldp.org/LDP/abs/html/options.html) set. Eventually `activate` breaks when the `errexit` option is also set:

```
$ /bin/bash -c 'source activate testenv; echo $CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV'
testenv
$ /bin/bash -eu -c 'source activate testenv; echo $CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV'
/home/jenkins-ci/miniconda3/bin/deactivate: line 55: CONDA_PATH_BACKUP: unbound variable
$ 
```

When sourced in a non-interactive session, `activate` does the same thing all by itself (even with `changeps1: False`):
```
$ /bin/bash -eu -c 'CONDA_PATH_BACKUP=""; source activate testenv; echo $CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV'
/home/jenkins-ci/miniconda3/bin/activate: line 65: PS1: unbound variable
$
```

It's not crazy to activate a conda env in such a shell. `nounset` and `errexit` are helpful and fairly common when debugging, and they help avoid silent failures in production. 

Some options are:

1. Ask users to activate conda environments in shells without `nounset` and `errexit` options set,
2. Bind these variables at the beginning of each script if they're unbound, or
3. Fix these two tests to [check for unbound variables](http://stackoverflow.com/a/13864829).

Option (3) seems least disruptive to me. If there are no objections, I'll open a PR soon.

issue_title: Bad interpreation of null in proxy_servers section in .condarc since ver. 4.2.13
issue_body:
Upon upgrade to conda to version 4.2.13 the following settings in .condarc, which were meant to unset any http_proxy or https_proxy variables no longer work; result in HTTP error.

```
proxy_servers:
    # Unset proxies so we can define internal channels shouldn't go outside anyway
    http:
    https:
```
Error received upon conda install:

```
An HTTP error occurred when trying to retrieve this URL.
ConnectionError(MaxRetryError("HTTPConnectionPool(host=u'None', port=80): Max retries exceeded with url: http://sfp-inf.jpmchase.net:9000/conda/mrad/channel/dev/linux-64/repodata.json (Caused by ProxyError('Cannot connect to proxy.', NewConnectionError('<requests.packages.urllib3.connection.HTTPConnection object at 0x7f197d701610>: Failed to establish a new connection: [Errno -2] Name or service not known',)))",),)
```

The reason is wrongly reinterpreting null values as 'None' string and subsequently trying to connect to hostname 'None'.

For those of you need a workaround now, change your .condarc as follows:

```
proxy_servers:
    # Unset proxies so we can define internal channels shouldn't go outside anyway
    http: ""
    https: ""
```

issue_title: conda install --use-local not working after conda-build --build-only
issue_body:
Hi,

Running `conda build --build-only my_package` does not put any tarballs into the `conda-bld` directory. I would have expected a build system to create a tarball as part of the output.

When `conda install --use-local my_package` runs, it looks in the `conda-bld` directory, but can't find the tarball and so fails. Of course, this problem doesn't come about if you just run `conda build my_package`. I suspect this is because it needs to install the package into a test environment, and thus the tarball would have to be created in the process.

I'm not sure if this behaviour is by design, or if I'm doing something else wrong, but I certainly didn't expect it.

issue_title: Conda local install does not respect dependencies of dependencies
issue_body:
I noticed that when installing a local package conda does not install all the necessary dependencies. Specifically it does not install the dependencies of the dependencies leaving the installed local package in a broken state.

For example if I'm trying to install a local package that depends on pandas, conda will correctly install pandas but not pytz which pandas depends on. In this scenario trying to import the modules from the local package simply results in an ImportError for the missing pytz libraries. 

I noticed the previous #466 issue but the problem with dependencies of dependencies is not mentioned there. Also the #992 PR mention this behaviour in the help but I don't understand the reason behind it.
